name: Lewis Carroll — A Genealogy of Wonderland
description: A context map of the world around the Alice books: the author Charles Dodgson / Lewis Carroll, the real Alice Liddell, the illustrators who answered Tenniel, and the companion works (Looking-Glass, Jabberwocky, the Snark). Built from free public-domain sources — bios via Wikipedia, portraits and plates via Wikimedia Commons (license + artist baked into every credit). A recursive-recording context grammar. Curation: PlayfulProcess; text AI-assisted and historically verified; all imagery public domain.

metadata.author: PlayfulProcess
metadata.source_note: Bios condensed from English Wikipedia (CC BY-SA text, paraphrased); every image is public domain, sourced from Wikimedia Commons or the British Library with artist + license recorded in each item's metadata.credit. No AI image generation was used. Cross-links point at ../alice-in-wonderland (editions + people) — reference only, not overwritten.

sections.Who: Charles Lutwidge Dodgson — pen name Lewis Carroll — was an English author, mathematician, photographer and reluctant Anglican deacon, lecturing in mathematics at Christ Church, Oxford. He was noted for his facility with word-play, logic and fantasy; his nonsense reflects his published work on mathematical logic.
sections.In this map: The origin point. He told the tale to the Liddell sisters on a river outing (1862), hand-wrote and illustrated it as *Alice's Adventures Under Ground* (1864), then expanded it into *Wonderland* (1865) and its sequel *Through the Looking-Glass* (1871), and wrote the nonsense poems *Jabberwocky* and *The Hunting of the Snark*.
sections.Connections: → told the story to **Alice Liddell** (muse) · → commissioned & closely directed **John Tenniel** · → wrote **Under Ground**, **Wonderland**, **Looking-Glass**, **Jabberwocky**, **The Hunting of the Snark**.

metadata.credit: Lewis Carroll (Charles L. Dodgson), self-portrait photograph, c. 1857. Public domain (PD-old, author d. 1898). Source: Wikimedia Commons, File:LewisCarrollSelfPhoto.jpg.

sections.Who: Alice Pleasance Liddell, daughter of the Dean of Christ Church, was the child to whom Lewis Carroll first told the Wonderland story during a boating trip, and the dedicatee of the book. She shared her name with the heroine, though scholars disagree on how far the character was 'based on' her.
sections.In this map: The muse and first audience. The 1864 manuscript *Alice's Adventures Under Ground* was a handwritten gift to her.
sections.Connections: → the story was told to her by **Lewis Carroll** · → received the **Under Ground** manuscript as a gift.

metadata.credit: Photograph of Alice Liddell by Lewis Carroll, c. 1858–1860. Public domain (PD-old, photographer d. 1898). Source: Wikimedia Commons, File:Alice_Liddell.jpg.

sections.Who: Sir John Tenniel was a leading English illustrator and political cartoonist (chief cartoonist of *Punch*). Knighted in 1893 — the first such honour for an illustrator — he defined the canonical look of Alice in 42 wood-engravings for *Wonderland* (1865) and again for *Looking-Glass* (1871).
sections.In this map: The image-maker every later illustrator answers to. A reluctant collaborator whom Carroll micromanaged. His Jabberwock and Humpty Dumpty are the definitive images of the sequel.
sections.Connections: → commissioned & directed by **Lewis Carroll** · → illustrated **Wonderland**, **Looking-Glass**, **Jabberwocky** · → the reference point for **Arthur Rackham** and the whole later illustrator lineage in the Alice performance.

metadata.credit: Portrait of Sir John Tenniel. Public domain (PD-old, sitter/illustrator d. 1914). Source: Wikimedia Commons, File:John_Tenniel.png.

sections.Who: Arthur Rackham was a leading figure of the Golden Age of British book illustration, known for robust pen-and-ink line combined with delicate watercolour. His 1907 *Alice* (Heinemann) was the first major illustrated edition after the British copyright lapsed.
sections.In this map: The most famous of the 'second wave' illustrators who reinterpreted Alice once the book entered the public domain — represented in the Alice performance alongside Walker, Le Fanu, Hudson, Woodward, Rountree and others.
sections.Connections: → works in the shadow of **John Tenniel** · → reinterprets **Wonderland** (1907) · → one node of the illustrator lineage in the **Alice performance**.

metadata.credit: Arthur Rackham, self-portrait. Public domain (PD-old, artist d. 1939; work pre-1929). Source: Wikimedia Commons, File:Arthur_rackham_selfportrait.jpg.

sections.What: The original handwritten, self-illustrated manuscript Carroll made as a gift for Alice Liddell in 1864 — the seed of the published book. Now British Library Add MS 46700.
sections.In this map: The root text: Carroll as his own first illustrator, before Tenniel. The recursive-recording Alice performance already uses pages from this manuscript as its oldest 'edition'.
sections.Connections: → written & drawn by **Lewis Carroll** · → gift to **Alice Liddell** · → expanded into **Wonderland**.

metadata.credit: Lewis Carroll, autograph manuscript of ‘Alice's Adventures Under Ground’ (1864), British Library Add MS 46700, f.18r. Public domain (PD-old, author d. 1898). Digitised by the British Library; via Wikimedia Commons.

sections.What: The published novel (Macmillan, 1865), expanded from *Under Ground* and illustrated by John Tenniel. Among the most important works of Victorian literature; a touchstone of literary nonsense.
sections.In this map: The book at the centre of the recursive-recording Alice performance — every illustrated edition from 1865 to 1933 is stamped onto a single LibriVox karaoke reading there.
sections.Connections: → written by **Lewis Carroll** · → illustrated by **John Tenniel** · → reinterpreted by **Arthur Rackham** & later illustrators · → sequel: **Through the Looking-Glass**.

metadata.credit: Sir John Tenniel, ‘The White Rabbit’, wood-engraving for *Alice's Adventures in Wonderland* (Macmillan, 1865). Public domain (PD-old, illustrator d. 1914). Source: Wikimedia Commons, File:Alice-white-rabbit.jpg.

items.name: Through the Looking-Glass, and What Alice Found There

sections.What: The 1871 sequel: Alice climbs through a mirror into a reversed world structured like a chess game, where logic runs backwards. It contains *Jabberwocky* and the Humpty Dumpty 'a word means what I choose it to mean' dialogue.
sections.In this map: The mirror book — its reversal theme rhymes with recursive.eco's own concerns. Tenniel illustrated it too.
sections.Connections: → written by **Lewis Carroll** · → illustrated by **John Tenniel** · → contains **Jabberwocky** · → sequel to **Wonderland**.

metadata.credit: Sir John Tenniel, ‘Alice and Humpty Dumpty’, from *Through the Looking-Glass* (Macmillan, 1871). Public domain (PD-old, illustrator d. 1914). Source: Wikimedia Commons, File:Alice_with_Humpty_Dumpty_(Illustration_by_John_Tenniel).png.

sections.What: A nonsense poem within *Through the Looking-Glass*, recounting the slaying of the Jabberwock. Famous for its coined portmanteau words ('slithy', 'brillig', 'mimsy') — invented language that nonetheless reads as grammatical.
sections.In this map: Carroll's clearest demonstration that *grammar* can carry meaning even when vocabulary is invented — a natural anchor for a 'grammar' platform. Tenniel's frontispiece Jabberwock is its defining image.
sections.Connections: → written by **Lewis Carroll** · → appears in **Through the Looking-Glass** · → illustrated by **John Tenniel** · → lends words & setting to **The Hunting of the Snark**.

metadata.credit: Sir John Tenniel, ‘The Jabberwock’, frontispiece to *Through the Looking-Glass* (1871). Public domain (PD-old, illustrator d. 1914). Source: Wikimedia Commons, File:Jabberwocky.jpg.

sections.What: A nonsense poem (1876), subtitled 'An Agony, in Eight Fits', telling of an absurd crew hunting an undefined creature, the Snark — which may turn out to be a Boojum. It borrows setting, creatures and portmanteau words from *Jabberwocky*. Illustrated by Henry Holiday.
sections.In this map: The far node of Carroll's nonsense — a whole voyage built from invented terms. Illustrated not by Tenniel but by Henry Holiday (1839–1927).
sections.Connections: → written by **Lewis Carroll** · → borrows from **Jabberwocky** · → illustrated by **Henry Holiday**.

metadata.credit: Henry Holiday, Plate 1 (‘The Landing’) for *The Hunting of the Snark* by Lewis Carroll (Macmillan, 1876). Public domain (PD-old; Holiday d. 1927, Carroll d. 1898). Source: Wikimedia Commons.

sections.What: The river outing on the Isis (Thames at Oxford) toward Godstow, 4 July 1862, when Carroll first improvised the Wonderland story for the three Liddell sisters — Alice begging him afterwards to 'write it out'. The 'golden afternoon' of the prefatory poem.
sections.In this map: The event the whole genealogy hangs from: an oral story, told once, that Alice asked to have written down — which became the manuscript, the books, the illustrations, and (a century and a half on) this performance.
sections.Connections: → **Lewis Carroll** improvised the tale · → for **Alice Liddell** & her sisters · → became **Alice's Adventures Under Ground**.

metadata.credit: Illustrated with a page of Carroll's own 1864 manuscript (British Library Add MS 46700, f.18r). Public domain (PD-old). Source: Wikimedia Commons / British Library. (No contemporary image of the boat trip itself is public domain; the manuscript stands in for the origin.)
